backfill_note: The experiments were executed on 2026-07-01 and the post published 2026-07-07; this run directory was packaged later (2026-07-22). What is preserved VERBATIM from the original run: the embedding host source (harness/host/src/main.rs) and the console transcripts in observed-output.txt. What is RECONSTRUCTED so a third party can re-run the method: the two guest programs (harness/guest_a.rs, harness/guest_cve.rs), the host Cargo.toml, and run.sh — re-authored from the run notes and the transcripts they produced, not byte-preserved originals. results.json reports the behavior observed on 2026-07-01. No hashes of the original binaries were captured at run time; that is stated here rather than fabricated.

environment.note: This is a permission-boundary check (is an operation allowed or refused, and what does the protected file contain afterwards), not a timing benchmark. CPU/RAM do not affect pass/fail and are omitted. Hard-link and rename semantics can differ subtly per host OS — the transcripts are what was observed on Windows 11.

subject.name: wasmtime
subject.install: official prebuilt release binaries for the CLI experiment; the `wasmtime` / `wasmtime-wasi` crates pinned to =46.0.0 and =46.0.1 for the embedding experiment
subject.note: Version pins are exact (`=46.0.0`, `=46.0.1`). The whole point is an A/B across a one-patch-version boundary, so `latest` would destroy the evidence.

tool.name: rustc + cargo + wasmtime
tool.language: Rust guests (wasm32-wasip1) + Rust embedding host + bash driver (run.sh)

raw_artifacts.retention_policy: tool binaries and cargo target/ discarded at run time (finite local disk); transcripts and host source retained
raw_artifacts.note: The wasmtime release binaries and the host's cargo target/ directory were deleted after the run per the firsthand-cleanup policy. observed-output.txt (the decisive evidence) and the host source were kept and are committed here. run.sh re-downloads the pinned binaries into a .gitignored work/ dir.
